article_title: Southernmost Purgatorius Fossils Unearthed in Colorado, Extending the Range of Earth's Earliest Known Primate Relative by 800 Kilometers

content_preview.title: Southernmost Purgatorius Fossils Unearthed in Colorado, Extending the Range of Earth's Earliest Known Primate Relative by 800 Kilometers
content_preview.summary: Paleontologists have recovered tiny fossilized teeth of Purgatorius from Colorado's Denver Basin, marking the southernmost occurrence ever documented and suggesting that archaic primates dispersed rapidly across western North America within hundreds of thousands of years of the dinosaur extinction.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Three minuscule fossilized teeth recovered from Colorado's Denver Basin have extended the known geographic range of [*Purgatorius*](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260303050619.htm) — the earliest documented relative of all primates, including humans — roughly 800 kilometers south of any previous find. The discovery, [published in the *Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology*](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02724634.2026.2614024) on 2 March 2026, indicates tha...

recommendations: 1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically
editor_notes:
Well-written paleontology news article covering a genuinely significant fossil discovery. The summary accurately reflects the article content. All key claims — southernmost Purgatorius occurrence, ~800 km range extension, screen-washing methodology, Corral Bluffs site, Dr. Chester and team, NSF funding, possible new species — are substantiated by the accessible sources. The article correctly contextualizes the find within the broader Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary recovery narrative. Word count (604) is appropriate for the News category.

source_verification:
- phys.org: CONFIRMED. Corroborates the Denver Basin discovery, Corral Bluffs location, screen-washing technique, Dr. Stephen Chester (Brooklyn College/CUNY) leadership, southernmost range extension, quote about small fossils being easily missed, and the implication that primates spread southward after mass extinction.
- sciencedaily.com: CONFIRMED. Confirms Purgatorius was arboreal (ankle bone evidence), previously known only from Montana and southwestern Canada, Dr. Chester's quote about small fossils, screen-washing methodology, and the sampling bias explanation for previous absence from southern sites.
- tandfonline.com (Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology): HTTP 403 (access restricted). This is the primary peer-reviewed source. The DOI structure is consistent with a legitimate Taylor & Francis publication. Cannot independently verify but the other sources reference this paper.
- eurekalert.org: HTTP 403 (access restricted). Press release service commonly used by universities; the URL pattern is consistent with a legitimate release. Other sources corroborate the claims attributed to this source (unique combination of features, possible new species).
